claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 6 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 3 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 6 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 3 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital/residential accommodation for 13 weeks (>12) and claimant is not regularly spending money on child
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 6 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Child in hospital/residential accommodation for 13 weeks (>12) and claimant is not regularly spending money on child
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child in hospital/residential accommodation for 13 weeks (>12) and claimant is not regularly spending money on child
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's start your Child Benefit eligibility assessment.

**First, I need to know about the child or children you're claiming for.**

How old is the child (or children) you want to claim Child Benefit for?

- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

If you have multiple children of different ages, please let me know about each of them and we can assess eligibility for all of them.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Oh hello, thanks for helping me with this! I've got two children actually - Alex who's 6 years old, and Blake who's 3. They're both under 16, so pretty young still!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! It's lovely to meet you, and thanks for sharing that information about Alex and Blake. How wonderful - two young children!

Since both Alex (6 years old) and Blake (3 years old) are under 16, they automatically meet the age requirement for Child Benefit. Let me move forward with the next step in the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
The next question is about whether the children are working. Given that Alex is 6 and Blake is 3, I think we can safely assume they're not working 24 or more hours per week! 

Can you confirm that neither Alex nor Blake is in paid work for 24 hours or more per week? (This would be a "yes" - they are working **less** than 24 hours per week or not working at all.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Ha! No, definitely not - they're both far too young for that! Neither of them works at all. Alex is in primary school and Blake is still just a toddler at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Ha, yes of course! I thought as much, but I need to ask all the questions to follow the process properly. 😊

So that's confirmed - neither Alex nor Blake is working, which means they meet this requirement. Let me continue to the next step.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Next question - again, this one is unlikely to apply given their ages, but I need to check:

**Are Alex and Blake NOT receiving any benefits in their own right?**

This would include things like Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit claimed by the child themselves. 

I'd expect the answer is "yes" (they are NOT receiving such benefits) given how young they are, but please confirm!

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - neither of them receives any benefits in their own name. They're just kids, so no Universal Credit or anything like that for them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, that's exactly what I expected! Thanks for confirming. Neither Alex nor Blake receives benefits in their own right, so they meet this requirement too.

Let me move to the next step.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Another quick check - and again, given their ages, this should be straightforward:

**Are Alex and Blake NOT in an apprenticeship?**

Certain apprenticeships can disqualify a child from Child Benefit (such as Intermediate, Advanced, Higher, or Degree Apprenticeships in England, or Modern Apprenticeships in Scotland). But at ages 6 and 3, I'm confident they're not apprentices!

Can you confirm neither child is in an apprenticeship?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, definitely not! They're way too young for apprenticeships. Alex is only 6 and in primary school, and Blake is just 3. No apprenticeships for either of them!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Absolutely right! No apprenticeships for a 6-year-old and a 3-year-old! 😄

That's another requirement met. Now let's move on to some questions about you as the claimant.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now we need to check your residency status. To be eligible for Child Benefit, you need to meet UK residency requirements.

**Do any of the following apply to you?**

1. **You normally live in the UK**
2. You have **settled status** under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have **pre-settled status** under the EU Settlement Scheme AND you're working/earning above the National Insurance threshold
4. You have **pre-settled status** AND you're actively looking for work (can claim for up to 91 days)
5. You have **pre-settled status** AND have sufficient financial resources (and not claiming certain means-tested benefits)
6. You have **pre-settled status** AND you're a student with sufficient resources
7. You have **pre-settled status** as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national with right to reside
8. You're a **Crown servant** living abroad (or in certain qualifying countries)

The most common answer is simply that you normally live in the UK. Does that apply to you, or does one of the other options fit your situation better?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I normally live in the UK - I live in Manchester with Alex and Blake.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Wonderful! Living in Manchester with Alex and Blake means you meet the residency requirement perfectly. Let's continue to the next step.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to check that you're responsible for Alex and Blake.

**Are you responsible for the children?** This can be met in any of the following ways:

1. **You live with the child** - This is the most common situation
2. **You contribute to their upkeep** - Paying at least the equivalent of Child Benefit towards their care (food, clothes, pocket money, housing, etc.)
3. **You pay maintenance** - Under a court order or agreement

Since you mentioned you live in Manchester with Alex and Blake, I assume you live with both children. Is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - both Alex and Blake live at home with me in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's confirmed - you live with both Alex and Blake, so you're responsible for them. Excellent!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now an important question about other potential claimants:

**Is anyone else currently claiming or planning to claim Child Benefit for Alex or Blake?**

For example, this could be another parent, a partner, or another family member. Only one person can receive Child Benefit for a child at any time.

- **No one else is claiming** for either child
- **Someone else was claiming, but we've agreed I should claim instead**
- **There's a dispute** about who should claim

Which of these applies to your situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, no one else is claiming Child Benefit for either of them. It's just me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, that makes things straightforward! No other claimants to worry about.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check where Alex and Blake are currently living.

**What is the living situation for your children?**

- **Normal living** - They live at home with you (the usual situation)
- **In hospital** - Currently staying in hospital
- **In care** - Currently in local authority care
- **Fostered** - Currently in a foster care arrangement

I expect the answer is "normal living" since you mentioned they're at home with you in Manchester, but please confirm!

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Alex lives at home with me normally - that's straightforward. But Blake is actually in hospital at the moment. He's been there for a while now, about three months or so.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Oh, I'm sorry to hear Blake is in hospital - I hope he's getting the care he needs and will be back home with you soon.

Since Alex and Blake have different situations, let me handle them separately. Alex is straightforward with normal living at home. For Blake, I need to check the hospital duration rules.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Blake has been in hospital for about 3 months (approximately 12-13 weeks).

There are special rules for children in hospital:
- Child Benefit normally continues for up to **12 weeks** while a child is in hospital
- If there have been multiple hospital stays with gaps of less than 28 days between them, those stays are added together cumulatively

Let me ask a few clarifying questions about Blake's situation:

1. **Has Blake been in hospital continuously for these 3 months, or has he had any breaks at home?**

2. If there were breaks, **how long were the gaps** between hospital stays?

This will help me work out if Blake is under or over the 12-week limit.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He's been in hospital continuously - no breaks at home during these three months. So that would be just over 12 weeks straight.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. So Blake has been in hospital continuously for just over 12 weeks, which means he's exceeded the 12-week limit.

However, there's still a way you could remain eligible for Child Benefit for Blake even after the 12-week period. Let me check the next requirement.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good news - there's an exception to the 12-week rule!

**Are you regularly spending money on Blake while he's in hospital?**

This could include things like:
- Buying him clothes, toys, or books
- Bringing him treats or snacks
- Paying for things he needs
- Any other regular spending for his benefit

If you're married or in a civil partnership and living with your partner, their spending on Blake would also count.

If you are regularly spending money on Blake, you can still continue to receive Child Benefit for him even though he's been in hospital for more than 12 weeks.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I haven't been able to spend money on his needs while he's been in there. It's been difficult financially.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I understand - that must be a really tough situation, both emotionally and financially. I'm sorry to hear you're going through this.

Unfortunately, because Blake has been in hospital for more than 12 weeks continuously and you haven't been able to regularly spend money on him during that time, I'm afraid you wouldn't be eligible for Child Benefit for Blake at this time.

However, let me now check the rest of the eligibility for **Alex**, who lives at home with you normally. Let me continue with his assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Now let me go back and complete Alex's assessment. Since he lives at home normally, I need to restart from the appropriate point for him.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now for the final question about **Alex**:

**Is the local council NOT paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words, is Alex living with you without any local council funding for his care or housing? (The answer is likely "yes" - the council is NOT paying - if he's simply living at home with you as his parent.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right - the council isn't paying anything towards Alex. He just lives at home with me as normal.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's the final check complete for Alex. Since the council isn't paying towards his accommodation or maintenance, and he meets all the other requirements, Alex qualifies for Child Benefit.

Let me now provide you with the full outcome for both children.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Your Child Benefit Eligibility Results

Here's a summary of your eligibility assessment:

---

### ✅ Alex (age 6) - **ELIGIBLE**

Great news! You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex. He meets all the requirements:
- Under 16 years old
- Not working
- Not receiving benefits in his own right
- Not in an apprenticeship
- You live in the UK
- You're responsible for him (he lives with you)
- No one else is claiming for him
- The council isn't paying for his care

---

### ❌ Blake (age 3) - **NOT CURRENTLY ELIGIBLE**

I'm sorry, but you're not currently eligible for Child Benefit for Blake because:
- He has been in hospital continuously for **more than 12 weeks**
- You have not been regularly spending money on him during this time

---

## Important Information for Blake's Situation

**This doesn't have to be permanent.** Blake's eligibility could change:

1. **If Blake comes home** - Once he leaves hospital and returns home, you can claim Child Benefit for him again. However, be aware that if he returns to hospital within 28 days, the time will be added to his previous stay.

2. **If you start regularly spending on Blake** - Even small, regular amounts spent on his needs (toys, clothes, treats, books) could mean you qualify for the exception. If your financial situation changes and you're able to do this, you may want to contact HMRC to reassess.

---

## Next Steps

1. **For Alex**: You can apply for Child Benefit at [www.gov.uk/child-benefit](https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit)

2. **For Blake**: I'd recommend contacting HMRC directly to discuss your situation - they may be able to advise on any exceptions that could apply, or help you understand what counts as "regularly spending" on Blake. You can reach them on **0300 200 3100**.

I hope Blake recovers soon and is back home with you and Alex. Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

reasonings: Alex (6 years old) meets all the eligibility requirements for Child Benefit: (1) He is under 16 years old, so automatically qualifies on age. (2) He is not working 24+ hours per week. (3) He is not receiving benefits in his own right. (4) He is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship. (5) The claimant normally lives in the UK (Manchester). (6) The claimant is responsible for Alex as he lives with them. (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. (8) Alex lives at home normally (not in hospital, care, or fostered). (9) The local council is not paying towards his accommodation or maintenance.

Blake (3 years old) is not currently eligible for Child Benefit. While he meets most requirements - he is under 16, not working, not receiving benefits, not in an apprenticeship, the claimant lives in the UK and is responsible for him, and no one else is claiming - Blake has been in hospital continuously for more than 12 weeks. Under the hospital rules, Child Benefit continues for up to 12 weeks while a child is in hospital. After 12 weeks, eligibility can continue only if the claimant is regularly spending money on the child. As the claimant confirmed they have not been able to spend money on Blake's needs during his hospital stay, the exception does not apply and Blake is not currently eligible.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has two children: Alex (6) and Blake (3). Alex is eligible for Child Benefit as he meets all standard requirements - living at home with the claimant in Manchester under normal circumstances. Blake is currently ineligible because he has been in hospital continuously for over 12 weeks, and the claimant has not been regularly spending money on him during this time, which means the exception to the 12-week hospital rule does not apply. However, if Blake returns home or if the claimant begins regularly spending on Blake, eligibility could be reassessed.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Has the child's cumulative time in hospital or residential accommodation exceeded 12 weeks?
response.help_text: Child Benefit continues for up to 12 weeks in hospital/residential accommodation. CUMULATIVE RULE: If child returns within 28 days of leaving, time counts towards 12-week limit. Multiple stays within 28 days are added together.

criteria.calculation_method: Sum all hospital/residential stays. If gap between stays is less than 28 days, count both stays towards cumulative total. If gap is 28+ days, reset counter.
criteria.example: 10 weeks hospital + 15 day gap + 3 weeks hospital = 13 weeks cumulative (exceeds limit)

age_limits.birthday_rule: Benefit stops on child's 20th birthday, not end of academic year

training_exclusions: Training with employer agreement for future job

payment_rates.note: Current rates not specified in source documents - check gov.uk for latest rates

high_income_charge.note: High Income Child Benefit Charge applies if you or partner earns over £60,000. This is a payment adjustment, not an eligibility criterion.

response.question: Are you regularly spending money on the child while they are in hospital/residential accommodation?
response.help_text: Exception to 12-week rule if you regularly spend money on child. For hospital abroad, only if they went abroad for treatment and you're back in UK. Partner's spending counts if married/civil partnered and living together.

response.reason: Child has been in hospital or residential accommodation for more than 12 weeks and you are not regularly spending money on them
response.guidance: Child Benefit stops after 12 weeks in hospital or residential accommodation, unless you regularly spend money on them. If child returns within 28 days of leaving, the time counts towards the 12-week limit. Contact HMRC to check if exceptions apply. Partner's spending only counts if married/civil partnered and living together.

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

(2) He is not working 24+ hours per week. (3) He is not receiving benefits in his own right. (4) He is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship. (5) The claimant normally lives in the UK (Manchester). (6) The claimant is responsible for Alex as he lives with them. (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. (8) Alex lives at home normally (not in hospital, care, or fostered). (9) The local council is not paying towards his accommodation or maintenance.

Blake (3 years old) is not currently eligible for Child Benefit. While he meets most requirements - he is under 16, not working, not receiving benefits, not in an apprenticeship, the claimant lives in the UK and is responsible for him, and no one else is claiming - Blake has been in hospital continuously for more than 12 weeks. Under the hospital rules, Child Benefit continues for up to 12 weeks while a child is in hospital. After 12 weeks, eligibility can continue only if the claimant is regularly spending money on the child. As the claimant confirmed they have not been able to spend money on Blake's needs during his hospital stay, the exception does not apply and Blake is not currently eligible.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant has two children: Alex (6) and Blake (3). Alex is eligible for Child Benefit as he meets all standard requirements - living at home with the claimant in Manchester under normal circumstances. Blake is currently ineligible because he has been in hospital continuously for over 12 weeks, and the claimant has not been regularly spending money on him during this time, which means the exception to the 12-week hospital rule does not apply. However, if Blake returns home or if the claimant begins regularly spending on Blake, eligibility could be reassessed.
